text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST — 18-MONTH POST-SURGICAL SURVEILLANCE

PATIENT: Denise Okafor
CLINICAL INDICATION: Surveillance imaging in a patient with history of left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, triple-negative, grade 3, stage IIB (T2N1M0), treated with neoadjuvant pembrolizumab/ddAC/paclitaxel followed by left modified radical mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection and right prophylactic mastectomy, adjuvant capecitabine and pembrolizumab (discontinued for immune-related hepatitis), and adjuvant olaparib (now completed). Post-mastectomy radiation therapy to left chest wall and regional nodes completed. Bilateral silicone implant reconstruction in place. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant carrier. Presenting for 18-month post-surgical surveillance.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast from 12 months post-surgery (most recent prior study).

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis acquired in the portal venous phase following the administration of 100 mL of intravenous iohexol contrast. Multiplanar reformats generated and reviewed. Chest acquired in the standard inspiratory phase. No oral contrast administered per protocol.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The central airways are patent. Trachea is midline. The bronchi are widely patent to the segmental level. There is a new, well-circumscribed, smoothly marginated soft tissue nodule in the right lower lobe measuring approximately 4 mm in greatest axial dimension (series 3, image 74), which was not present on the prior examination from 12 months post-surgery. The nodule is subpleural in location, without satellite nodules, cavitation, or associated ground-glass opacity. No other discrete pulmonary nodules are identified. No areas of consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or interstitial abnormality are noted. No pleural effusions or pleural thickening bilaterally.

Chest wall and axillae: Bilateral silicone breast implants are in place, with no evidence of implant rupture, capsular contracture, or periprosthetic fluid collection. Post-mastectomy surgical changes are noted in the left chest wall, with expected soft tissue remodeling and subcutaneous fibrous stranding in the region of the prior mastectomy flap. Post-radiation changes persist in the left chest wall soft tissues, appearing stable compared with prior—mild subcutaneous fat stranding and skin thickening in the expected radiation field without new or progressive abnormality. The right chest wall shows post-surgical changes from prophylactic mastectomy consistent with prior imaging. No chest wall mass or soft tissue nodularity is identified in either chest wall to suggest locoregional recurrence.

Lymph nodes: No pathologically enlarged mediastinal, hilar, or supraclavicular lymph nodes are identified. No anterior mediastinal nodal masses. Left axillary surgical bed shows expected post-dissection changes with a small amount of residual fibrofatty tissue; no discrete nodal mass or suspicious adenopathy is seen. Right axilla is unremarkable. No internal mammary chain lymphadenopathy.

Heart and pericardium: Heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The visualized coronary arteries show no gross calcification.

Aorta and great vessels: The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber and contour. No mediastinal vascular abnormality.

Bones (thoracic): No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesion in the visualized thoracic spine, ribs, sternum, or clavicles. Mild degenerative changes at the thoracic facets, age-appropriate.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size, with smooth contours and homogeneous parenchymal enhancement. No focal hepatic lesion identified. No intrahepatic biliary dilatation. Prior imaging had demonstrated hepatic parenchyma affected in the setting of immune-related hepatitis; the liver now appears entirely normal in morphology with no residual signal abnormality or parenchymal distortion to suggest ongoing hepatic inflammation or fibrosis.

Biliary system: Gallbladder is present, with no gallstones or wall thickening. No pericholecystic fluid. Common bile duct not dilated (3 mm).

Spleen: Normal in size and homogeneous in enhancement. No focal splenic lesion.

Pancreas: The pancreas enhances uniformly throughout the head, body, and tail. No ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding or focal mass.

Adrenal glands: Both adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal nodule or mass.

Kidneys: The kidneys are symmetric in size and enhancement, with prompt and symmetric excretion of contrast. No hydronephrosis. No renal mass. No renal calculi.

Gastrointestinal: Visualized bowel loops are unremarkable without evidence of wall thickening, obstruction, or adjacent adenopathy. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites.

Retroperitoneum and lymph nodes: No retroperitoneal, mesenteric, or para-aortic lymphadenopathy. The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber. No abdominal vascular abnormality.

Bones (abdomen/lumbar): No suspicious osseous lesion. Mild facet arthropathy at L4-L5, age-appropriate. No compression deformity.

---

PELVIS:

Uterus: Normal in size and contour. No adnexal mass. Ovaries are visualized bilaterally and appear grossly unremarkable in size and morphology; no complex adnexal lesion. (Note: Bilateral risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy has been discussed but not yet performed per clinical record.)

Urinary bladder: Distended and smooth-walled. No intraluminal lesion.

Pelvic lymph nodes: No pelvic sidewall or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Bones (pelvis): Cortex intact throughout the pelvis, sacrum, and visualized proximal femora. No lytic or blastic lesion to suggest osseous metastatic disease.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of locoregional recurrence in the bilateral chest walls, axillae, or regional lymph node stations.

2. New 4 mm smoothly marginated right lower lobe pulmonary nodule not present on prior examination 12 months ago. No additional pulmonary nodules or associated features to suggest malignant etiology. Given the patient's oncologic history and small nodule size, short-interval follow-up CT of the chest in approximately 3 months is suggested for further characterization; in the absence of interval growth, a benign etiology such as an intrapulmonary lymph node or small granuloma would be favored.

3. No hepatic lesions. Liver morphology normal, with complete radiographic resolution of previously noted inflammatory changes attributed to immune checkpoint inhibitor hepatitis.

4. No suspicious pulmonary, hepatic, osseous, adnexal, or nodal abnormality elsewhere to suggest distant metastatic disease.

5. Bilateral silicone breast implants intact. Post-mastectomy and post-radiation changes in the left chest wall are stable.

Radiologist: [Electronically signed]
Dictated and finalized.
